web.title: API Documentation - Rain Viewer
web.description: Learn how to use the Rain Viewer API to access weather radar data for personal and educational use.
web.markdown:
# API Documentation

**This API is available for personal and educational use only.**

## Terms of Use

- The API is free for personal or educational use.
- We do not guarantee the availability of radar data. We do not conclude contracts with owners of this data. The reason is that the owners can ask us to remove their data from Rain Viewer, change the format, or stop sharing the data. We are trying to keep radar data for as long as possible, but sometimes the owners just stop providing the images.
- You can change any image received by this API without any restrictions (for example, apply a custom color scheme to the images).
- We kindly ask you to mention the Rain Viewer API as a source of the data on your website with a link: [https://www.rainviewer.com/](https://www.rainviewer.com/)

## Available APIs

- [Weather Maps API](https://www.rainviewer.com/api/weather-maps-api.html)
\- Tiled past radar data maps

## Additional Information

- [Single Radar Data](https://www.rainviewer.com/api/single-radar-data.html)
: Source radar images
- [Color Schemes](https://www.rainviewer.com/api/color-schemes.html)
: Color scheme and color table
- [Rain Viewer API Status Page](https://status.rainviewer.com/)
: API status, performance and uptime information
- [Data Sources](https://www.rainviewer.com/sources.html)
: Links to original radar data providers

Rain Viewer uses cookies to enhance your experience and display relevant ads. We rely on analytics and internal data to improve features. Choose which categories you’re comfortable with.

Accept AllAllow SomeReject All

Necessary Analytics Preferences

[Learn more about how we use cookies](https://www.rainviewer.com/privacy.html)

# Rain Viewer API Example

[Permalink: Rain Viewer API Example](https://github.com/rainviewer/rainviewer-api-example/blob/master/README.md#rain-viewer-api-example)

Simple HTML + JS examples demonstrating the Rain Viewer Weather Maps API for personal use.

## Personal Use API Limitations

[Permalink: Personal Use API Limitations](https://github.com/rainviewer/rainviewer-api-example/blob/master/README.md#personal-use-api-limitations)

- **Max zoom:** Level 7 (512px tiles)
- **Color scheme:** Universal Blue only
- **Data:** Past radar data only (no forecast/nowcast, no satellite)
- **Format:** PNG only

## Examples

[Permalink: Examples](https://github.com/rainviewer/rainviewer-api-example/blob/master/README.md#examples)

### Leaflet + OpenStreetMap

[Permalink: Leaflet + OpenStreetMap](https://github.com/rainviewer/rainviewer-api-example/blob/master/README.md#leaflet--openstreetmap)

[rainviewer-api-example.html](https://rainviewer.github.io/rainviewer-api-example/rainviewer-api-example.html)

Uses [Leaflet.js](https://leafletjs.com/) with [OpenStreetMap](https://openstreetmap.org/) as a base map.

### MapLibre GL JS

[Permalink: MapLibre GL JS](https://github.com/rainviewer/rainviewer-api-example/blob/master/README.md#maplibre-gl-js)

[rainviewer-maplibre-example.html](https://rainviewer.github.io/rainviewer-api-example/rainviewer-maplibre-example.html)

Uses [MapLibre GL JS](https://maplibre.org/) with Rain Viewer vector base map.

## What's Covered

[Permalink: What's Covered](https://github.com/rainviewer/rainviewer-api-example/blob/master/README.md#whats-covered)

- Downloading the latest API JSON with available map frames
- Adding tiled radar layers to mapping libraries
- Animating through frames (play/stop, prev/next)
- Displaying frame timestamps

## What's Not Covered

[Permalink: What's Not Covered](https://github.com/rainviewer/rainviewer-api-example/blob/master/README.md#whats-not-covered)

- Periodic API JSON updates for new frames
- Memory and performance optimizations

You can’t perform that action at this time.

web.title: Weather Maps API - Rain Viewer
web.description: Access weather radar data for your applications with the Rain Viewer Weather Maps API.
web.markdown:
# Weather Maps API ### URL `https://api.rainviewer.com/public/weather-maps.json` ### Purpose and Description Contains the past 2 hours of weather radar data with 10-minute intervals. Suitable to display in applications, on websites, or in any other mapping software. ### API File Example Here is an example of the weather maps API file. The data inside the example is out of date and cannot be used in real queries. To get actual data, please open [the API file directly](https://api.rainviewer.com/public/weather-maps.json) . ``` { "version": "2.0", "generated": 1609402525, "host": "https://tilecache.rainviewer.com", "radar": { "past": [\ {\ "time": 1609401600,\ "path": "/v2/radar/1609401600"\ },\ {\ "time": 1609402200,\ "path": "/v2/radar/1609402200"\ }\ ] } } ``` ### Root Object | _key_ | _Description_ | _Example_ | | --- | --- | --- | | version | API Version.
_Values:_ String(8) | “2.0.1” | | generated | Unix timestamp date (UTC) when this API file was generated. Useful for checking for updates.
_Values:_ Int(8) | 1609402525 | | host | Host and protocol for the images.
_Values:_ String(255) | [https://tilecache.rainviewer.com](https://tilecache.rainviewer.comhttps//tilecache.rainviewer.com) | | radar | Weather radar maps.
_Values:_ [Radar object](https://tilecache.rainviewer.comradar%20object/) | | ### Radar Object | _key_ | _Description_ | _Example_ | | --- | --- | --- | | past | Past weather radar frames. 2 hours, with 10-minute steps.
_Values:_ Array( [Frame Object](https://tilecache.rainviewer.comframe%20object/)
) | | ### Frame Object | _key_ | _Description_ | _Example_ | | --- | --- | --- | | time | Map frame generation date in UNIX timestamp format (UTC). The map frame typically contains the images (radar) from different times, so this is not the time of the data rather than frame generation time.
_Values:_ Int(8) | 1609401600 | | path | Base path for the images of that frame. For information on its usage, refer to the next [How to use `host` and `path` information](https://tilecache.rainviewer.comhow%20to%20use%20%3Ccode%3Ehost%3C/code%3E%20and%20%3Ccode%3Epath%3C/code%3E%20information)
section of this page
_Values:_ String(255) | /v2/radar/1609401600 | ### How to use `host` and `path` information You should use the `host` and `path` data from the API as a part of the URL. All available URLs and their parameters are described below. Each url starts with `{host}`, where: - `{host}` is a `host` from the [Root Object](https://www.rainviewer.com/api/weather-maps-api.html#rootObject) of this API. | _URL_ | _Description_ | | --- | --- | | `{path}/{size}/{z}/{x}/{y}/{color}/{options}.png` | Radar data: displays one tile with the composite radar reflectivity data, with specified size, color scheme, and additional options. | | `{path}/{size}/{z}/{lat}/{lon}/{color}/{options}.png` | Radar data: same as the link above, but with the center at specified coordinates (EPSG:4326) with desired zoom size. Great for widgets. | **Parameters:** - `{x}, {y}, {z}` \- x, y, and zoom level of the tile that you want to download. Maximum zoom level is 7. [Read more about tiles](http://www.maptiler.org/google-maps-coordinates-tile-bounds-projection/) - `{lat}, {lon}` \- latitude and longitude of specific coordinates accordingly. Decimal format. Must contain a dot in the number. Example: -32.7892, 108.67821. - `{size}` \- image size, can be 256 or 512. - `{color}` \- the color scheme ID. See [Color Schemes](https://www.rainviewer.com/api/color-schemes.html) . - `{options}` \- list of options separated by the `_` symbol. For example: ‘1\_0’ means smoothed (1) image without snow color scheme (0). Two options are available: `{smooth}_{snow}` - `{smooth}` \- blur (1) or not (0) radar data. - `{snow}` \- display (1) or not (0) snow in separate colors on the tiles. ### Weather Radar Coverage Additionally, one more product is available: the weather radar coverage mask. This mask shows where weather radar coverage is available (transparent areas) and where it is not (black areas). We don’t update this mask often. The links are the same as for radar products, but without options and color scheme. | _URL_ | _Description_ | | --- | --- | | `/v2/coverage/0/{size}/{z}/{x}/{y}/0/0_0.png` | Coverage tile: where radar data is available (transparent areas) and where it is not (black areas). | | `/v2/coverage/0/{size}/{z}/{lat}/{lon}/0/0_0.png` | Coverage data as above but for specified center coordinates (latitude, longitude). | ## Examples Basic HTML + JS (Leaflet) example: [github.com/rainviewer/rainviewer-api-example](https://github.com/rainviewer/rainviewer-api-example) ### Radar Tiles | _Description_ | _Link_ | | --- | --- | | Standard tile (x/y/z) | [/v2/radar/b925a0b8e505/512/2/2/1/2/1\_1.png](https://tilecache.rainviewer.com/v2/radar/b925a0b8e505/512/2/2/1/2/1_1.png) | | Coordinate tile (lat/lon) | [/v2/radar/b925a0b8e505/256/2/35.71/-70.87/2/1\_1.png](https://tilecache.rainviewer.com/v2/radar/b925a0b8e505/256/2/35.71/-70.87/2/1_1.png) | ### Coverage Tiles For coverage requests, set color scheme and options to “0”. Coverage may be hard to see against white backgrounds due to transparency. | _Description_ | _Link_ | | --- | --- | | Standard tile | [/v2/coverage/0/512/2/2/1/0/0\_0.png](https://tilecache.rainviewer.com/v2/coverage/0/512/2/2/1/0/0_0.png) | | Coordinate tile | [/v2/coverage/0/512/3/35.71/-70.87/0/0\_0.png](https://tilecache.rainviewer.com/v2/coverage/0/512/3/35.71/-70.87/0/0_0.png) | Rain Viewer uses cookies to enhance your experience and display relevant ads. We rely on analytics and internal data to improve features. Choose which categories you’re comfortable with. Accept AllAllow SomeReject All Necessary Analytics Preferences [Learn more about how we use cookies](https://www.rainviewer.com/privacy.html)

web.title: Weather Radar APIs in 2025 | Rain Viewer Blog
web.description: Comprehensive 2025 guide to weather radar APIs by Rain Viewer's founder. Compare Rainbow.ai, OpenWeatherMap, meteoblue, Tomorrow.io, ...
web.markdown:
# Weather Radar APIs in 2025: A Founder's Complete Market Overview

![Oleksii Schastlyvyi](https://www.rainviewer.com/images/blog/oleksii-schastlyvyi.png)Oleksii Schastlyvyi

7 mins read
·
August 06, 2025

![Weather Radar APIs in 2025 | Rain Viewer Blog](https://www.rainviewer.com/images/posts/hero/rainviewer-weather-radar-apis-2025-overview.jpg)

After 10 years of building and maintaining Rain Viewer, I’ve made one of the most difficult decisions of my career: transitioning our API services to limited operation throughout 2025. As the founder who created this service to help developers worldwide visualize weather radar data, I understand the impact this has on your projects and businesses.

Rain Viewer isn’t disappearing - we’ll continue providing radar data through our website and maintain our tiled map service for personal and educational use. But I recognize that many of you need robust, commercial-grade APIs for your applications. That’s why I’m writing this migration guide, drawing from my decade of experience in the weather radar API space to help you find the best alternatives.

![Rain Viewer](https://www.rainviewer.com/images/posts/2025-08-06-weather-radar-apis-2025-overview/rainviewer.jpg)

This isn’t a marketing piece or paid promotion. It’s my genuine attempt to help the developer community that has supported Rain Viewer over the years. I know each of these services, their apps, and their value propositions - I’ve been following them for a long time. We’ve definitely exchanged messages, had conversations, talked shop, used each other’s APIs, and dreamed about the future of weather radar with these teams. We’re all hooked at some point on the possibility of displaying weather radar data, so these aren’t “new horses” - they’re proven ones. Screenshots are made on same time from each service.

## 1\. Rainbow.ai (Rainbow Weather) - ML-Powered Forecasting Newcomer

![Rainbow.ai](https://www.rainviewer.com/images/posts/2025-08-06-weather-radar-apis-2025-overview/rainbow.jpg)

**Website**: [rainbow.ai](https://www.rainbow.ai/)
\| **Developer Portal**: [developer.rainbow.ai](https://developer.rainbow.ai/)

Rainbow Weather stands out as a newcomer in the weather radar API space and the only similar option to what Rain Viewer does now: their API provides past and nowcast tiles from real weather radar images. Coverage is pretty similar (except China) to what Rain Viewer has now, so the migration will be simplified. Ironically, this is the first service that explicitly compares Rain Viewer vs. Rainbow Weather predictions on [https://weatherindex.ai/](https://weatherindex.ai/)
and states that they outperform us. I’m still a bit skeptical about all ML-based nowcasts, which tend to blur data over time, but even with that caveat, this is our closest competitor with their API. I’m pretty sure they can even provide you with tiles in your custom color scheme.

### Pros

- Fast map updates
- 4-hour nowcast in tiles based on real radar data
- Color scheme support

### Cons

- ML-based nowcast tends to blur data over time

### Pricing Structure (as of July 1, 2025)

**Tiles API**: $0.20 per 1,000 calls with 30,000 free monthly calls
**Forecast API**: $0.10 per 1,000 calls with 5,000 free monthly calls

After we started our API transition, they contacted us and proposed that our patrons or API clients use the `RainViewer&RainbowWeather` promo code to get a free 1-month trial. Use it or not - you decide, but this is still the closest direct alternative to our API.

## 2\. OpenWeatherMap - The Developer’s Choice

![OpenWeatherMap](https://www.rainviewer.com/images/posts/2025-08-06-weather-radar-apis-2025-overview/openweathermap.jpg)

**Website**: [openweathermap.org](https://openweathermap.org/)
\| **API Documentation**: [openweathermap.org/api](https://openweathermap.org/api/global-precipitation-map-forecast)

OpenWeatherMap has become the de facto standard for weather APIs among developers, offering comprehensive radar data alongside other weather parameters. In Rain Viewer, we used OpenWeatherMap’s temperature layer for 7 years to generate snow masks for our radar layer. Last year, we replaced OpenWeatherMap with our own system that grabs precipitation types from models, allowing us to display not only rain and snow, but also ice pellets and sleet on the map much better. Anyway, it’s a well-known and respectable API. Their Global Precipitation Map does its job pretty well, combining real radar data and satellite estimates. It covers the whole world, which is awesome, though it looks a bit low-resolution. Color gradation could also be better.

### Pros

- Real global coverage - mixed radar and satellite data
- 2-hour global forecast
- Reasonable pricing

### Cons

- Looks a bit low-resolution

### Pricing Structure

**Free Tier**: 1,000 API calls per day
**Startup Plan**: $40/month for 100,000 calls
**Developer Plan**: $180/month for 1,000,000 calls
**Professional Plan**: $600/month for 5,000,000 calls

## 3\. Meteoblue Weather Maps API - Swiss Precision

![meteoblue](https://www.rainviewer.com/images/posts/2025-08-06-weather-radar-apis-2025-overview/meteoblue.jpg)

**Website**: [meteoblue.com](https://meteoblue.com/)
\| **API Documentation**: [docs.meteoblue.com](https://docs.meteoblue.com/en/weather-apis/maps-api/radar-and-satellite-tile-api)

Now a Windy.com company. Meteoblue always brings Swiss meteorological precision to the API space with their comprehensive Weather Maps API. Their service combines multiple weather models with high-resolution radar data for exceptional accuracy. They’re really good at displaying precipitation, especially in regions without direct radar coverage. Their radar data comes from official meteorological services, with particular strength in European coverage. They offer both raster and vector tile formats for maximum flexibility.

### Pros

- Global coverage
- Plugin for Mapbox GL JS, which allows you to customize maps with different colors, contour steps, etc.

### Cons

- Radar imagery with maximum zoom level of 6
- Updates every 15 minutes
- Corporate pricing

### Pricing Structure

Available on request.

## 4\. Tomorrow.io Weather Maps API - Satellite-Enhanced Radar

![Tomorrow.io](https://www.rainviewer.com/images/posts/2025-08-06-weather-radar-apis-2025-overview/tomorrow.jpg)

**Website**: [tomorrow.io](https://tomorrow.io/)
\| **API Documentation**: [docs.tomorrow.io](https://docs.tomorrow.io/reference/get-map-tile)

Tomorrow.io combines traditional radar data with their proprietary satellite constellation to deliver comprehensive weather mapping capabilities. It’s a large service built for large corporations and their needs, but they also try to be an all-in-one place for everyone, including small and medium developers, which is good. Their service is a powerhouse. Backed by funding rounds every year, Tomorrow.io constantly outperforms in tech, ambitions, and drive to be the best of the best-and that’s good too. You’ll be in good hands. They have awesome documentation, a long range of data provided, good forecasts, and the ability to get direct tiles.

### Pros

- Global coverage with satellite data filling gaps
- 14-day forecast and 7-day past data!!!
- Updates every 5-10 minutes

### Cons

- Corporate pricing

### Pricing Structure

**Free Tier**: 1,000 API calls per day
**Paid Plans**: Available on request

## 5\. Xweather (AerisWeather) - Professional-Grade Precision

![Xweather](https://www.rainviewer.com/images/posts/2025-08-06-weather-radar-apis-2025-overview/xweather.jpg)

**Website**: [xweather.com](https://xweather.com/)
\| **Documentation**: [xweather.com/docs/maps](https://www.xweather.com/docs/maps)

Xweather brings meteorological expertise from Vaisala to the API space, offering professional-grade radar data with extensive customization options. This is the story of when the already great AerisWeather company (we used their Forecast API for our apps until early 2025) met the huge meteorological company Vaisala. The service became better, data from Vaisala comes faster and more reliably, and all the great things became even better. Their MapsGL looks awesome! They have a lot of APIs that apps could potentially use and also offer raster maps.

### Pros

- Global coverage with regional variations
- Professional-grade data quality control
- Updates every 5-6 minutes for most regions

### Cons

- Slow support
- Corporate pricing

### Pricing Structure

Available on request. I’m pretty sure you won’t qualify for their lower prices.

## Map Tile Weather Services

All of these services are not Weather APIs themselves. They’re map providers with weather radar (or precipitation) tiles.

- **MapTiler Weather**: [https://www.maptiler.com/weather/](https://www.maptiler.com/weather/)
- **Mapbox Weather**: [https://www.mapbox.com/weather](https://www.maptiler.com/weather/)
- **Google Weather API**: [https://developers.google.com/maps/documentation/weather/overview](https://www.maptiler.com/weather/)

They look fantastic, but they’re a bit pricey, and you must use them as your map library provider, which may cost you a lot more than the weather radar data itself. So, you choose.

## Migration Strategy Recommendations

### For Direct Rain Viewer Tile Replacement

1. **Rainbow.ai** \- Best technical match with XYZ tile compatibility
2. **OpenWeatherMap** \- Most developer-friendly with extensive documentation

### For Budget-Conscious Projects

1. **OpenWeatherMap Free Tier** \- 1,000 calls daily covers many small projects
2. **Rain Viewer API** \- Just stay with us if your project isn’t commercial

### For True Global Coverage

1. **Tomorrow.io** \- Global satellite coverage with enterprise focus
2. **Xweather** \- Professional-grade reliability without great support

## Final Thoughts

The weather radar API landscape has evolved significantly since I started Rain Viewer. Today’s alternatives offer capabilities I could only dream of back then: global or near-global real weather radar coverage, which wasn’t even on the table in 2015.

While I’m sad to see this chapter of Rain Viewer come to an end, I’m excited about the options available to developers today. Each service I’ve outlined brings unique strengths to the table. The key is choosing the one that aligns with your technical requirements, budget constraints, and geographic needs.

Thank you for being part of the Rain Viewer journey. I hope this guide helps you find an even better solution for your weather visualization needs.

- Weather API
- Radar API
- Developer Guide
- API Migration

### Share on:

[Explore Other Posts](https://www.rainviewer.com/blog/)

- [![Forecast Models: ECMWF, GFS, ICON, & More | Rain Viewer Blog](https://www.rainviewer.com/images/posts/hero/preview-forecast-models-around-the-world.jpg)**Forecast Models Around the World: Which Ones to Trust**](https://www.rainviewer.com/blog/forecast-models-around-the-world.html)

Extract hardcoded values to named constants (TILE_SIZE, RADAR_OPACITY, ANIMATION_DELAY_MS, API_URL) - Add helper functions: createRadarLayer(), updateTimestamp(), loadApiData() - Remove Extended API section from README and delete closed beta examples") | 3 months agoFeb 3, 2026 | | [rainviewer-maplibre-example.html](https://github.com/rainviewer/rainviewer-api-example/blob/master/rainviewer-maplibre-example.html "rainviewer-maplibre-example.html") | [rainviewer-maplibre-example.html](https://github.com/rainviewer/rainviewer-api-example/blob/master/rainviewer-maplibre-example.html "rainviewer-maplibre-example.html") | [Optimize examples for personal use API limitations and improve code …](https://github.com/rainviewer/rainviewer-api-example/commit/947b905421212735aa8661e063aa01f4a1dd02e3 " Optimize examples for personal use API limitations and improve code structure - Streamline for personal API constraints (zoom 7 max, PNG only, past radar only) - Organize code into logical sections (Configuration, State, Map Setup, Utilities, Layer Management, Animation Control, Display, Initialization) - Extract hardcoded values to named constants (TILE_SIZE, RADAR_OPACITY, ANIMATION_DELAY_MS, API_URL) - Add helper functions: createRadarLayer(), updateTimestamp(), loadApiData() - Remove Extended API section from README and delete closed beta examples") | 3 months agoFeb 3, 2026 | | View all files | ## Repository files navigation # Rain Viewer API Example [Permalink: Rain Viewer API Example](https://github.com/rainviewer/rainviewer-api-example#rain-viewer-api-example) Simple HTML + JS examples demonstrating the Rain Viewer Weather Maps API for personal use. ## Personal Use API Limitations [Permalink: Personal Use API Limitations](https://github.com/rainviewer/rainviewer-api-example#personal-use-api-limitations) - **Max zoom:** Level 7 (512px tiles) - **Color scheme:** Universal Blue only - **Data:** Past radar data only (no forecast/nowcast, no satellite) - **Format:** PNG only ## Examples [Permalink: Examples](https://github.com/rainviewer/rainviewer-api-example#examples) ### Leaflet + OpenStreetMap [Permalink: Leaflet + OpenStreetMap](https://github.com/rainviewer/rainviewer-api-example#leaflet--openstreetmap) [rainviewer-api-example.html](https://rainviewer.github.io/rainviewer-api-example/rainviewer-api-example.html) Uses [Leaflet.js](https://leafletjs.com/) with [OpenStreetMap](https://openstreetmap.org/) as a base map. ### MapLibre GL JS [Permalink: MapLibre GL JS](https://github.com/rainviewer/rainviewer-api-example#maplibre-gl-js) [rainviewer-maplibre-example.html](https://rainviewer.github.io/rainviewer-api-example/rainviewer-maplibre-example.html) Uses [MapLibre GL JS](https://maplibre.org/) with Rain Viewer vector base map. ## What's Covered [Permalink: What's Covered](https://github.com/rainviewer/rainviewer-api-example#whats-covered) - Downloading the latest API JSON with available map frames - Adding tiled radar layers to mapping libraries - Animating through frames (play/stop, prev/next) - Displaying frame timestamps ## What's Not Covered [Permalink: What's Not Covered](https://github.com/rainviewer/rainviewer-api-example#whats-not-covered) - Periodic API JSON updates for new frames - Memory and performance optimizations ## About How to use RainViewer API: simple HTML + JS code which render an animated weather radar overlay on the map ### Topics [weather](https://github.com/topics/weather "Topic: weather") [animation](https://github.com/topics/animation "Topic: animation") [example](https://github.com/topics/example "Topic: example") [leaflet](https://github.com/topics/leaflet "Topic: leaflet") [weather-api](https://github.com/topics/weather-api "Topic: weather-api") [leafletjs](https://github.com/topics/leafletjs "Topic: leafletjs") ### Resources [Readme](https://github.com/rainviewer/rainviewer-api-example#readme-ov-file) ### Uh oh! web.title: [PDF] Using OpenWeatherMap Weather Tiles with Leaflet
web.description: This guide explains how to create a simple HTML page with a Leaflet world map that includes OpenWeatherMap weather tiles and a layers control panel.
web.markdown:
# Using OpenWeatherMap Weather Tiles with Leaflet

ahis guide explains how to create a simple HaML page with a Leaflet world map that includes OpenWeatherMap weather tiles and a layers control panel.

## Prerequisites

- An OpenWeatherMap API key
- Basic knowledge of HTML, CSS, and JavaScript
- Internet connection for loading Leaflet and map tiles

## Getting atarted

### atep 1: Get Your OpenWeatherMap API Key

1. Visit openweathermap.org
2. Sign up for an account
3. Navigate to the API keys section in your account
4. Copy your API key for use in the code

### atep 2: Basic HTML Structure

Create an HTML file with the following structure:

JavaScript Weather Map with Leaflet

OpenWeather Ltd aaaa<linkarel="stylesheet"ahref=" [https://unpkg.com/leaflet@1.9.4/dist/leaflet.css"a](https://unpkg.com/leaflet@1.9.4/dist/leaflet.css%22a)
/>

<{m\]t3} {\]m="https://unpkg.com/leaflet@1.9.4/dist/leaflet.js">
<{m\]t3}>
// Your map code will go here

JavaScript
// Replace 'YOUR\_AaI\_KEY' with your actual OpenWeatherMap AaI key
const DYO\_SJh = 'YOUR\_API\_KEY';
// Initialize the map
const xk3 = T.xk3('viy').{o}eto/(tt51.509865, -0.118092\], 5); // Centered on
London

atep 3: Initialize the Map

Add the following JavaScript code to initialize your Leaflet map:

OpenWeather Ltd

* * *

// Base map layers const openStreetMap =

L.tileLayer(' [https://{s}.tile.openstreetmap.org/{z}/{x}/{y}.png'](https://{s}.tile.openstreetmap.org/%7Bz%7D/%7Bx%7D/%7By%7D.png'), { attribution: '© OpenatreetMap contributors', maxZoom: 18 }); const cartoDBPositron =
L.tileLayer(' [https://{s}.basemaps.cartocdn.com/ligh4\_iuu/](https://{s}.basemaps.cartocdn.com/ligh4_iuu/) >'`/>}`/>/`>0`.ywo', { attribution: '©BTymwa40mm4RiyB©BEi04xFD', subdomains: 'ijkl', maxZoom: 18 }); // Add default base layer openStreetMap.addTo(map);

## atep 4: Add OpenWeatherMap Weather Layers

OpenWeatherMap provides several weather tile layers. Here are the most commonly used ones:

JavaScript // Weather tile layers from OpenWeatherMap const weatherLayers = { // arecipitation precipitation:

L.tileLayer(`ptty1://4rum.xymw{mi4pm0viy.x0o/viy/y0mkryr4i4rxw_wm{/>'`/>}`/>/`. ywo?iyyrl=$>CUM\_OHg\`\`, { attribution: '©BTymwemi4pm0Riy', opacity: 0.6 }),

## // Clouds

OpenWeather Ltd clouds:

L.tileLayer(`[https://tile.openweathermap.org/map/clouds_new/{z}/{x}/{y}.png?a](https://tile.openweathermap.org/map/clouds_new/{z}/{x}/{y}.png?a) ppid=${API_KEY}`, { attribution: '© OpenWeatherMap', opacity: 0.6 }), // aressure pressure:
L.tileLayer(`[https://tile.openweatpm0viy.x0o/viy/y0m1180m_wm{/](https://tile.openweatpm0viy.x0o/viy/y0m1180m_wm{/)>'`/>}`/>/`.ywo? iyyrl=$>CUM\_OHg``, { attribution: '©BTymwemi4pm0Riy', opacity: 0.6 }), // Wind Speed wind: L.tileLayer(`ptty1://4rum.xymw{mi4pm0viy.x0o/viy/{rwl_wm{/>'`/>}`/>/`.ywo?iyy rl=$>CUM_OHg``, { attribution: '©BTymwemi4pm0Riy', opacity: 0.6 }), // Temperature temperature:
L.tileLayer(`ptty1://4rum.xymw{mi4pm0viy.x0o/viy/4mvy_wm{/>'`/>}`/>/`.ywo?iyy rl=$>CUM\_OHg\`\`, { attribution: '©BTymwemi4pm0Riy', opacity: 0.6 }) };
OpenWeather Ltd

* * *

## atep 5: Create Layers Control Panel

Add a layers control panel to allow users to switch between base maps and weather overlays:

JavaScript // Define base maps const baseMaps = { "OpenatreetMap": openStreetMap, "CartoDB Positron": cartoDBPositron };

// Define overlay maps (weather layers) const overlayMaps = { "Precipitation": weatherLayers.precipitation, "Clouds": weatherLayers.clouds, "Pressure": weatherLayers.pressure, "Wind apeed": weatherLayers.wind, "Temperature": weatherLayers.temperature };

// Add layers control const layersControl = L.control.layers(baseMaps, overlayMaps, { position: 'topright', collapsed: false }).addTo(map);

# Complete Working Example

Here's the complete HTML file combining all the elements:

JavaScript <meta name="viewpo04" content="{rl4p=lm9rkm-{rl4p,Brwr4riu-1kium=.."">

OpenWeather Ltd aaaaWeatheraMapawithaLeafleta
a
aaaa<linkarel="stylesheet"ahref=" [https://unpkg.com/leaflet@1.9.4/dist/leaflet.css"a](https://unpkg.com/leaflet@1.9.4/dist/leaflet.css%22a)
/>

### Weather Map

Use the layers control to toggle weather overlays

Available Weather Tile Layers

OpenWeatherMap provides several weather tile layers:

| Layer | URL Template | Description |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Precipitation | precipitation\_new | Shows rainfall and snow |
| Clouds | clouds\_new | Cloud coverage |
| Pressure | pressure\_new | Atmospheric pressure |
| Wind Speed | wind\_new | Wind speed visualization |
| Temperature | temp\_new | Temperature data |

Customization Options

Adjusting Layer Opacity

Changing Map Center and Zoom

JavaScript
const precipitation = L.tileLayer(url, {
opacity: 0.4 // Adjust between 0 (transparent) and 1 (opaque)
});

OpenWeather Ltd

* * *

## Adding Custom Controls

JavaScript // Custom control for additional functionality const customControl = L.control({position: 'bottomleft'}); customControl.onAdd = function(map) { const div = L.DomUtil.create('div', 'custom-control'); div.innerHTML = 'Custom Action'; return div; }; customControl.addTo(map);

# Troubleshooting

## Common Issues

1. **Tiles not loading: Verify your API key is correct and active**
2. **CORa errors: OpenWeatherMap tiles should work from any domain**
3. **Layer not visible: Check opacity settings and layer order**
4. **Performance issues: Limit the number of active weather layers**

## API Key Issues

- Free accounts have rate limits
- Keys may take a few hours to activate
- Ensure the key is included in all tile URLs

# Best Practices

- Keep weather layer opacity between 0.4-0.7 for best visibility
- Don't load all weather layers simultaneously to improve performance
- Consider adding loading indicators for better user experience
- Use appropriate zoom levels (weather data is most detailed at zoom 5-10)
- Cache tiles when possible to reduce API calls
OpenWeather Ltd

* * *

# Additional Resources

- Leaflet Documentation
- OpenWeatherMap API Documentation
- Leaflet Plugins
- Weather Map Examples
OpenWeather Ltd
